sense: stay fluent while you think (any part)
def: buys a moment to plan before a Part 3 answer
example: That’s an interesting question — I’ve never really thought about it.

sense: stay fluent while you think (any part)
word: Let me think for a second
def: signals you are thinking, not stuck, without dead silence
example: Let me think for a second… I suppose the main reason is cost.

sense: stay fluent while you think (any part)
def: natural opener that earns thinking time and sets up nuance
example: Well, it depends — for some people it’s great, for others not so much.

sense: stay fluent while you think (any part)
word: Off the top of my head
def: introduce a quick, unprepared answer
example: Off the top of my head, I’d say convenience is the biggest factor.

sense: stay fluent while you think (any part)
def: buys time while you search for the right wording
example: How can I put this… it’s a bit of a double-edged sword.

sense: stay fluent while you think (any part)
def: flags that the real point is coming
example: The thing is, most people simply don’t have the time.

def: casual, natural way to introduce a personal opinion
example: If you ask me, social media does more harm than good.

def: soft, fluent way to commit to a view
example: I’d say education is by far the most important factor.

def: signal a personal stance to open an answer
example: Personally, I think working from home suits most people.

def: emphatic way to mark a firm personal opinion
example: As far as I’m concerned, it’s the government’s responsibility.

def: strong, confident statement of a value or view
example: I’m a firm believer in learning by doing.

def: soften an opinion so it sounds measured, not dogmatic
example: I tend to think people worry about it more than they need to.

def: emphatic agreement; stronger than ‘I agree’
example: I couldn’t agree more — it’s exactly what I was thinking.

def: concede before disagreeing — sounds balanced
example: That’s a fair point, but I still think the benefits win out.

word: I see what you mean, however
def: acknowledge then push back politely
example: I see what you mean, however the evidence suggests otherwise.

word: I’m not so sure about that
def: disagree gently without being blunt
example: I’m not so sure about that, to be honest.

word: It’s not as simple as that
def: challenge an over-simple claim and add nuance
example: It’s not as simple as that — there are trade-offs either way.

def: agree partially, leaving room to qualify
example: I agree up to a point, but it has its downsides too.

def: express near-certainty about the future
example: Prices are bound to keep rising over the next few years.

def: state a strong probability
example: There’s a good chance remote work will become the norm.

def: express strong doubt about something happening
example: It’s highly unlikely things will go back to how they were.

def: soft speculation when you’re unsure
example: I imagine most people would prefer the convenience.

def: draw a likely inference; sounds analytical
example: Presumably, that’s why younger people are leaving the area.

def: formal hedge for a confident prediction
example: In all likelihood, technology will solve part of the problem.

def: add a further point; more natural than ‘moreover’ in speech
example: It’s cheap, and on top of that it’s really convenient.

def: introduce the key point or a complication
example: The thing is, not everyone can afford it.

def: spoken linker for an extra, stronger point
example: It saves time, and what’s more, it’s better for the planet.

def: signal a contrast with what you just said
example: I love the city. Having said that, it can get exhausting.

def: mark the final stage of a sequence (better than ‘and then’)
example: We got lost, walked for hours, and eventually found the place.

def: introduce a consequence in a clear chain
example: I started exercising, and as a result I sleep far better now.

def: introduce a specific example; varies from ‘for example’
example: Take my brother, for instance — he works entirely from home.

def: spotlight one case to prove a general point
example: Take big cities, for example — the cost of living is huge.

word: a good example (of this) would be
def: set up a clear illustration
example: A good example would be how quickly phones replaced cameras.

def: introduce a hypothetical example
example: Let’s say you move abroad — you’d have to start from scratch.

def: give a personal example to support a claim
example: In my case, learning a language opened a lot of doors.

def: lead into a short personal anecdote (great for Part 2)
example: I remember when I first tried it — I was terrible at it.

def: express strong enjoyment of a hobby or topic
example: I’m really into hiking — I go most weekends.

def: express strong dislike; vivid and natural
example: I can’t stand being stuck in traffic.

word: it’s not really my cup of tea
def: say you’re not keen on something, idiomatically
example: Opera’s not really my cup of tea, to be honest.

def: state a clear preference between options
example: I’d much rather read a book than watch TV.

def: describe a preference that developed over time
example: I’ve grown to love cooking since I moved out.

def: express enthusiasm for something
example: I’m a big fan of live music — there’s nothing like it.

def: describe a past habit that has stopped
example: I used to collect stamps when I was a kid.

def: frame a memory in a past period
example: Back when I was at school, we barely used computers.

def: present perfect for a lifelong trait or habit
example: I’ve always been a bit of a night owl.

def: react to a nostalgic topic naturally
example: That song really brings back memories of my teens.

def: hedge a memory you’re not 100% sure of
example: If I remember rightly, it was about ten years ago.

def: anchor a feeling or fact to a past moment
example: At the time, I had no idea how much it would matter.

def: the standard, clean way to frame your Part 2 topic
example: I’d like to talk about a trip that really stuck with me.

def: highlight the memorable detail of an experience
example: What stood out was how friendly everyone was.

word: the reason it was so memorable
def: set up the ‘why’ bullet point
example: The reason it was so memorable is that I’d never done anything like it.

def: introduce background before the main event
example: To set the scene, it was the middle of winter and freezing.

def: reflect to round off and fill the full two minutes
example: Looking back, it taught me to be more independent.

def: wrap up the long turn with an overall impression
example: All in all, it was one of the best decisions I’ve made.

def: contrast two ideas in one sentence
example: City life is fast-paced, whereas the countryside is calmer.

def: set one thing against another
example: Compared to ten years ago, everything is far more digital.

def: open a sentence marking a clear difference
example: Older generations saved more. By contrast, many young people spend freely.

def: emphasise a big gap between two things
example: It’s nowhere near as expensive as people assume.

def: introduce the opposite angle or downside
example: The flip side is that it can be quite isolating.

def: frame a balanced comparison of pros and cons
example: There’s a trade-off between convenience and privacy.

def: soften or emphasise a frank opinion
example: To be honest, I’ve never really enjoyed it.

def: strong agreement or emphasis on an adjective
example: It was absolutely freezing that day.

def: emphatic way to introduce a strong feeling
example: What really gets me is how wasteful it all is.

def: flag a surprising fact and add interest
example: Believe it or not, I’d never travelled before then.

def: stress sincerity; a natural intensifier
example: I was genuinely amazed by how good it was.

def: single out the strongest reason or feeling
example: More than anything, it gave me confidence.
